text: The surgeon uses a uterine manipulator to move the uterus to the contralateral side, exposing the avascular triangle of freedom. This area is bounded by the round ligament superiorly, the external iliac artery and vein laterally, and the gonadal vessels medially. The ovarian vessels are grasped and gently pulled medially and superiorly, increasing the distance from the iliac vessels and causing peritoneal tenting.

The surgeon uses a uterine manipulator to direct the uterus to the contralateral side, exposing the avascular triangle of freedom. This area is bounded by the round ligament superiorly, the external iliac artery and vein laterally, and the gonadal vessels medially. The ovarian vessels are grasped with gentle medial superior traction to further expose the triangle, creating distance from the iliac vessels and causing peritoneal tenting.

The surgeon uses a gentle probe and spread technique to take superficial bites, exposing the pillars and bridges of adipose areolar and peritoneal tissue. Counter traction is adjusted continuously to reveal the ureter lying on the posterior peritoneum of the broad ligament, becoming more superficial towards the pelvic brim.

The surgeon uses a gentle probe and spread technique to take superficial bites, exposing the pillars and bridges of adipose areolar and peritoneal tissue. The ureter is visible lying on the posterior peritoneum of the broad ligament, becoming more superficial towards the pelvic brim. The probe and spread technique is continued to safely approach the ureter's location.

The surgeon identifies the ureter's location and proceeds to safely take down the anterior peritoneum, extending the cranial peritoneal incision to further dissect the ureter and potentially create a pedicle window.
The surgeon identifies the ureter's location and proceeds to take down the anterior peritoneum, extending the cranial peritoneal incision to further dissect the ureter and create a pedicle window.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis on congenital adhesions on the left side of the pelvis to medialize the colon. The mesentery of the colon is grasped with medial inferior countertraction, and bursts of monopolar energy are applied to safely move the bowel medially. This exposes the triangle of freedom, allowing for safe entry into the retroperitoneum.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis on the left side of the pelvis to medialize the colon, using bursts of monopolar energy while applying medial inferior countertraction on the mesentery. This exposes the triangle of freedom, allowing for safe entry into the retroperitoneum.

The surgeon uses a gentle probe and spread technique, adjusting the countertraction on the ovarian vessels to expose the iliac vessel landmarks. The ureter is identified lateral and inferior to these vessels, lying on the posterior peritoneum. The anterior peritoneum is opened parallel to the ovarian vessels, and the areolar tissue is dissected to further expose the ureter.

The surgeon uses a gentle probe and spread technique with constant adjustment of ovarian vessel countertraction to expose the iliac vessel landmarks. The ureter is identified lateral and inferior to these vessels, lying on the posterior peritoneum. The anterior peritoneum is opened parallel to the ovarian vessels, and areolar tissue is dissected to further expose the ureter.

The surgeon adjusts countertraction medial to the ovary to perform caudal ureolysis. The uterine vessels are visible anterior to the ureter, illustrating the 'water under the bridge' where the ureter passes beneath these vessels. The parietal space is developed, bounded by the ureter medially, the internal iliac laterally, and the uterine vessels caudally.

The surgeon adjusts countertraction medial to the ovary, facilitating further caudal ureolysis. The uterine vessels are visible anterior to the ureter, demonstrating the 'water under the bridge' configuration where the ureter passes beneath the uterine vessels. The parietal space is developed, bounded by the ureter medially, the internal iliac laterally, and the uterine vessels caudally.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection to skeletonize the uterine vessels for safe ligation, positioned lateral to the ureter. Following the transection of the round ligament, the anterior and posterior peritoneum are taken separately to ensure persistent identification and lateralization of the ureter, enhancing the skeletonization of the uterine artery.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection to skeletonize the uterine vessels for safe ligation lateral to the ureter. Following the transection of the round ligament, the anterior and posterior peritoneum are taken separately to ensure persistent identification of the ureter, allowing for safe ureterolateralization and improved skeletonization of the uterine artery.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm prograsp to lift the uterus out of the pelvis, incorporating the round ligament, and exposes the triangle of freedom. The ovarian vessels are grasped with medial traction, allowing entry into the retroperitoneum. The incision is extended parallel to the ovarian vessels, and the medial leaf of the broad ligament is identified and followed inferiorly towards the ureter.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm prograsp to lift the uterus out of the pelvis by grasping the cornea of the uterus and incorporating the round ligament, thereby exposing the triangle of freedom. The ovarian vessels are grasped with medial traction, allowing entry into the retroperitoneum, and the incision is extended parallel to the ovarian vessels.

The surgeon identifies the medial leaf of the broad ligament and follows it inferiorly towards the ureter. Upon identifying the ureter, the peritoneal incision is extended, and a peritoneal window is created medial to the ureter and lateral to the ovarian vessels to safely ligate and transect the ovarian vessels.

The surgeon extends the peritoneal incision and creates a peritoneal window medial to the identified ureter and lateral to the ovarian vessels, preparing to safely ligate and transect the ovarian vessels.
The surgeon develops the vesicle vaginal space prior to dissecting the posterior aspect of the broad ligament, preparing the surgical field for further dissection.
The surgeon develops the vesicle vaginal space before proceeding to take down the posterior of the broad ligament, preparing the area for further dissection.
The surgeon further dissects the ureter, ensuring it is at a safe distance from the site of uterine artery ligation before transecting the posterior peritoneum.
The surgeon further dissects the ureter, ensuring it is at a safe distance from the site of uterine artery ligation before transecting the posterior peritoneum.
